summary: O'Neil highlights two blog posts by young women in STEM: one by MIT student Michelle G. about intelligence, gender, and stereotype threat, and another by Harvard student Meena Boppana about her experiences as a female math competition winner. O'Neil uses these posts to amplify women's voices in male-dominated fields while adding her own commentary, including a pointed jab at Larry Summers and an acknowledgment that despite her skepticism of math contests, she appreciates what Boppana gained from them. The post also promotes an upcoming panel on gender inclusivity in mathematics at Harvard.
key_points: Young women in STEM are producing valuable writing about their experiences with gender and intelligence
Stereotype threat remains a relevant issue that even experts can learn more about
Women's experiences in competitive math spaces deserve attention and amplification
O'Neil maintains nuanced views—appreciating individual experiences while critiquing systemic structures like math contests
money_quotes: And who doesn't love nerdy young women!?
I thought I knew all about that stuff but I learned quite a bit from her post.
p.s. Larry Summers, I hope you read this.
Even though I personally think math contests kind of suck, I appreciate how much Meena got out of them.
key_insight: Amplifying young women's voices in STEM—and engaging critically with their experiences—is itself an act of pushing back against gender bias in these fields.
